bull.b: S&P Global’s very high and stable margins reflect pricing power and scale in ratings, indices and data subscriptions. Persistently wide gross and operating margins support durable cash generation, allow reinvestment and shareholder returns, and insulate earnings through moderate demand swings.

Near-conversion of earnings into free cash flow provides sustainable funding for buybacks, M&A and product investment. High FCF conversion reduces reliance on external financing, improving balance-sheet flexibility and enabling predictable capital allocation over the medium term.

Recurring subscriptions, index-linked fees and ratings fees create a diversified mix less dependent on single markets. Growth across Market Intelligence and Indices shows multiple stable cash engines, lowering overall business volatility and supporting sustained organic growth as markets evolve.

bear.b: Slowing revenue growth constrains operating leverage and future margin expansion. With growth rates below historical highs, management must rely more on margin improvement and M&A to hit targets, increasing execution risk and making sustained EPS upside more dependent on non-organic levers.

Ratings revenue is issuance‑sensitive and can swing with capital markets cycles; anticipated moderation and potential negative quarters highlight structural volatility. Earnings and cash flow can be lumpy, complicating planning and making medium‑term forecasts vulnerable to macro and issuance timing.

Carving out Mobility refocuses S&P on benchmarks and analytics but reduces revenue diversification and shifts consolidated cash flows. Separation, TSAs and Mobility’s standalone debt issuance alter capital allocation, leverage dynamics and near‑term free cash flow available for buybacks or investments.
